                 EMPLOYMENT
                 a n d EARNINGS
                                                    VOL- 1 7          NO. 12 J U N E


                 Joseph M. Finerty, Editor
                 John E. Bregger, Associate Editor


                           Editors' Note


                           Employment and Earnings, United States, 1909-70, BLS Bulletin 1312-7 is being released
                           this month by the Bureau of Labor Statistics. Based on the establishment survey, the bul-
                           letin contains historical data at the national level on industry employment, hours, earn-
                           ings, and labor turnover. All series are shown from the earliest date available through
                           February 1970. Data have been adjusted to March 1969 benchmarks and are comparable
                           to data appearing in Employment and Earnings beginning with the June 1970 issue (Vol.
                           16 No. 12). The bulletin may be purchased for $4.25 from the BLS regional offices.
                           (See last page of this issue for details on ordering.)
                           Beginning with this issue of Employment and Earnings, tables C-11 through C-15 will in-
                           clude additional detail on changes in average hourly earnings adjusted to exclude the ef-
                           fects of interindustry employment shifts and overtime premiums. Previously available
                           only for manufacturing, the data are now compiled for the total private nonfarm sector
                           and separately for each industry division. (The effects of overtime are still excluded only
                           for manufacturing establishments.) In the expansion of this series, previously published
                           data for manufacturing have been revised.

                                         In addition to the monthly data appearing regularly in Employment and
                                         Earnings, special features appear in most of the issues, as shown below:
                                                                               Jan.    Feb.   Mar.   Apr.   May   June   July   Oct.
                               Household data
                             Annual averages                                    X
                             Revised seasonally adjusted series and
                               current seasonal factors                                 X

                               Establishment data
                             National annual averages:
                               Industry divisions (preliminary)                 X
                               Industry detail (final)                                         X
                             State and area annual averages                                                  X
                             Area definitions                                                                X
                             National data adjusted to new benchmarks                                             (1)

                                current seasonal factors                                                          (1)

                               -1
                               Data shown in this issue are adjusted to March 1969 benchmarks, first published in the June
                           1970 issue. It is expected that data adjusted to March 1970 benchmarks will be introduced in the
                           August 1971 issue.

                      conduit craftsmen; and laborers. In 1968, such employees made up 32 percent of the total number of nonsupervisory employees in establishments reporting hours

                         1
                            The private economy includes government enterprises which cover over half their current operating expenses by the sale of goods and services, e.g.. Post Office, T V A , State liquor stores, etc. This comprises about
                   10 percent of total civilian government employment.
                         2
                            Man-hours refer to hours paid for all persons—wage and salaried workers, self-employed, unpaid family workers and private household workers—and are based largely on establishment data.
                         3
                            Wages and salaries of employees plus employers' contributions for social insurance and private benefit plans. Also includes an estimate of wages, salaries, and supplementary payments for the self-employed.
                         4
                            Compensation per man-hour adjusted for changes in the Consumer Price Index.
                         5
                            Nonlabor payments include profits, depreciation, interest, rental income, and indirect taxes.
                         6
                            Percent change computed from original data.
                         7
                           Current quarter divided by comparable quarter a year ago.

                                                                                       Job Vacancies
                 gates are the product of average weekly hours and production-
                 worker employment, and the payroll aggregates are the product               Job vacancies are the stock of unfilled job openings as of the
                 of gross average weekly earnings and production-worker employ-          close of the last business day of the reference month. Openings
                 ment.                                                                  for all kinds of positions, classifications and employment, full-
                                                                                        time, part-time, permanent, temporary, and seasonal are in-
                                                                                        cluded. Excluded are jobs to be filled by recall f r o m layoff,
                 Labor Turnover                                                         transfer, promotion, demotion or return from paid or unpaid
                                                                                       leave; jobs unoccupied because of labor-management disputes;
                    Labor turnover is the gross movement of wage and salary            job openings for which "new" workers were already hired and
                workers into and out of employed status w i t h respect to indi-       scheduled to start work later; and openings with future starting
                vidual establishments. This movement, which relates to a calen-        dates, which are requested as a separate item.
                dar month, is divided into t w o broad types: accessions (new               Job vacancies are defined as vacant jobs which are immedi-
                hires and rehires) and separations (terminations of employ-             ately available for filling, and for which the firm is actively try-

                                                                 Seasonal                 Adjustments

                        Many economic statistics reflect a regularly recurring sea-        approximation based on past experience. Seasonally adjusted
                  sonal movement which can be estimated on the basis of past               estimates have a broader margin of possible error than the
                  experience. By eliminating that part of the change which can             original data on which they are based, since they are subject
                  be ascribed to usual seasonal variation, it is possible t o ob-          not only t o sampling and other errors but, in addition, are
                  serve the cyclical and other nonseasonal movements in the                affected by the uncertainties of the seasonal adjustment proc-
                  series. However, in evaluating deviations f r o m the seasonal           ess itself. Seasonally adjusted series for selected labor force
                  p a t t e r n — t h a t is, changes in a seasonally adjusted series—     and establishment data are published regularly in     Employment
                  it is important t o note that seasonal adjustment is merely an           and Earnings.
